Paper Title: Application to the PRIMATE ACTION FUND for the research project: “Identification, distribution, taxonomy, and conservation of three populations of galagos (Primates, Galagidae) in Malawi: Malawi galago (Galagoides nyasae)& Mt. Thyolo galago, Mulanje Mountains and Thyolo Mountains; Kalwe small galago (Galagoides sp. nov. 1), Kalwe Forest Reserve; and the Misuku Hills galago (Galagoides sp.), Misuku Hills."
Relevant Excerpt:
1. Abstract:
 This study will further underpin a long-term study of Malawi's (and Tanzania's) Galagoides species phylogeny and conservation status that is planned to be undertaken by the Nocturnal Primate Research Group, Oxford Brookes University, starting in 2010.

2. Abstract:
 Identification, distribution, taxonomy, and conservation of three populations of galagos (Primates, Galagidae) in Malawi: Malawi galago (Galagoides nyasae)& Mt. Thyolo galago, Mulanje Mountains and Thyolo Mountains; Kalwe small galago (Galagoides sp. nov. 1), Kalwe Forest Reserve; and the Misuku Hills galago (Galagoides sp.), Misuku Hills."

3. Summary:
 This study aims to establish baseline data on presence/absence, taxonomic status, geographic spread, habitat preferences, and conservation status of four target populations in Malawi: Malawi galago, Galagoides nyasae;
Abstract: African nocturnal primates of the family Galagidae (galagos) display species-specific sets of vocalizations that can contain up to 25 structurally distinct calls. This fact has been used to identify populations in the wild and has been especially effective when studying and the small (<200 grams) and elusive members of the Galagoides genera. There are currently 11 species of Galagoides recognized but the taxonomic rank of at least three is uncertain due to lack of behavioral and genetic data. Five Galagoides species can be found in Malawi, where of four are endemic: Malawi galago (Galagoides nyasae), Mt. Thyolo galago (G. sp. nov. 2), Kalwe small galago (Galagoides sp. nov. 1), and the Misuku Hills galago (Galagoides sp.), but their taxonomic rank is highly uncertain or undecided. The current conservation status of these three populations is tentative since the studies that identified them dates back 20 (Kalwe small and Misuku Hills galago) to 120 (Malawi galago) years, and no further field study has been carried out since. In the light of the described data deficiency, this study suggests a survey of the above described Galagoides species' type localities, to record each population's vocal repertoire, map their geographical distribution, local abundance, and establish their basic ecological preferences. The baseline data derived from this study will help to clarify the taxonomic status, the presence and abundance, the ecological requirements, and allow an up-to-date analysis of their conservation status. This study will further underpin a long-term study of Malawi's (and Tanzania's) Galagoides species phylogeny and conservation status that is planned to be undertaken by the Nocturnal Primate Research Group, Oxford Brookes University, starting in 2010.

Paper Title: Identification, Abundance, and Behaviour of Galagos (Primates, Galagidae) in the Shire Highlands, Malawi
Relevant Excerpt:
1. BACKGROUND:
 Reviews of galago diversity highlight a lack of data for acknowledged species as well as potential new forms  and, in many cases, information regarding species-specific abundance, behaviour, ecology, and conservation status is incomplete or non-existent .

2. BACKGROUND:
 Given that Malawi's Shire Highlands are characterised by exceptionally high levels of human disturbance in contracting forest and woodland habitat , it may be anticipated that galagos across the region are subjected to considerable and increasing ecological pressure and warrant conservation attention.

3. INTRODUCTION:
 G. nyasae, and   G. granti) appear to replace each other sequentially from southern and western Tanzania through Malawi and into Mozambique, although each may co-occur with other species of galagos within their range .
Abstract: Although galagos are extensively distributed across sub-Saharan Africa as a taxonomic group, many species are only partially described both in terms of and throughout their range. In particular, there is a lack of data for species occurring in the Shire Highlands of Malawi, and one form at Thyolo Mountain awaits description. The aims of the study where to address existing data deficiencies by (i) identifying and describing the species of galago occurring in Malawi's Shire Highlands, (ii) assessing their distribution and relative abundance, (iii) providing insights into their behaviour, and (iv) gathering information with regard to human impacts that impinge upon their current and future conservation status. Assessment of the inter-relationships between these factors is fundamental to ensuring the long-term viability of galago populations in the region. Vocalisation analysis, compilation of call profiles, descriptions of morphology, systematic surveys, behaviour observations, and assessments of substrate-use were completed to identify two species of galago in the Shire Highlands (Galagoides granti and Otolemur crassicaudatus), determine their distribution and relative abundance on the basis of encounter rates, construct activity budgets, and compile substrate-use profiles that reflect habitat preferences across the region. Vocal repertoires were compiled for each species, and preliminary information regarding human impacts and disturbance was acquired. The results of the study not only contribute to clarification of galago diversity but also provide valuable preliminary data fundamental to management and conservation of galagos across the Shire Highlands.

Paper Title: Hidden in the Dark: A Review of Galagid Systematics and Phylogenetics
Relevant Excerpt:
1. Bridging systematics and conservation:
 Careful investigation of genetic diversity across the distribution of galagos will likely reveal narrow-range genotypically and morphologically unique lineages that may require special protection or management.

2. Authors: Anna Penna,Luca Pozzi (Corresponding Author),Galago • Bushbaby,Taxonomy • Systematics,Phylogenetics • Africa
Abstract: An accurate representation of species diversity is critical in primatology; most of the questions in evolutionary biology, ecology, and conservation hinge on species as a fundamental unit of analysis. Galagos are among the least-known primates. Because of their cryptic morphology, broad distribution, and sampling challenges arising from elusive habits and political instability, substantial knowledge gaps about their taxonomy, evolutionary history, and biogeography remain. Despite these limitations, recent research that integrated field surveys, acoustic, morphological, and genetic analyses helped us to better understand the taxonomic diversity of this primate group. In this paper, we (1) review the current status of galagid taxonomy; (2) synthesize our current understanding of their phylogenetics, origins, and biogeography; and (3) explore current and future approaches to elucidate galagid cryptic species diversity. The onset of galago systematics dates back to the early 19th century, with taxonomic descriptions following natural history expeditions and comparative anatomy studies. Although morphology has historically dominated systematic research on galagos, the coupling of acoustic analyses with genetic data has revolutionized the field. Taxonomic rearrangements include the discovery of new species in the wild (e.g., Galagoides kumbirensis) and the description of a new genus (Paragalago). Technological advances have allowed the collection of acoustic data in remote areas, and molecular techniques have the potential to help researchers fill important geographic gaps. Improving the resolution of galago species diversity also has implications for the conservation of wild populations, as a better understanding of species boundaries and ranges can aid in the implementation of conservation strategies.

Paper Title: Molecular phylogeny of East African dwarf galagos (Galagoides spp.)
Relevant Excerpt:
1. Objective:
 We therefore suggest a survey of specific localities in the Eastern Afromountain Biodiversity Hotspots of Tanzania, Kenya and Malawi to collect genetic material from all known and all presumptive dwarf galago species (table 1; see table 2 for timeline for fieldwork).
Abstract: Non-human primate species belong to a group of generally well studied mammals. One of the more pertinent exceptions is the rarely seen but often heard African nocturnal primate, the dwarf galago (genus Galagoides). As with many nocturnal animals, dwarf galagos have a cryptic morphology that confounds full understanding of their true species diversity. To fully quantify the extent of the taxonomic variety of the genus, it is necessary to conduct an assessment of the genetic diversity in an evolutionary perspective, i.e. create a genetically based phylogeny. However, current records of dwarf galago DNA are very patchy and are mostly derived from captive populations, leaving incomplete phylogenetic understanding. Differences between species are often hard to discern, especially when it comes to cryptic species such as dwarf galagos. Morphology and behavior cannot always be used to differentiate between species and cannot, with any high accuracy, reveal ancestral relationships between species. We therefore suggest a survey of specific localities in the Eastern Afromountain Biodiversity Hotspots of Tanzania, Kenya and Malawi to collect genetic material from all known and all presumptive dwarf galago species (table 1; see table 2 for timeline for fieldwork). The intent is to construct the most comprehensive phylogeny made to date over the genus Galagoides derived from genetic markers. We believe the results from this study will prove pivotal for future research and conservation prioritizing of dwarf galagos.

Paper Title: PHYLOGENETIC AND POPULATION GENETIC STUDIES OF NOCTURNAL PRIMATES IN AFRICAN FOREST COMMUNITIES (F.382E)
Publication Title: The Leverhulme Trust Annual Report No.2
Author Names:
Simon K Bearder
Paul E Honess
Relevant Excerpt:
1. INTRODUCTION:
 At the end of our second year of the project, fieldwork in Tanzania has indicated a remarkable diversity of galago populations, including previously unrecognised species in exmmely threatened habitats. All the populations examined to date have been found in tropical, evergreen forest in coastal, lowland and montane locations.

2. Abstract:
 Several of the forests contained species that we did not recognise, and these have been earmarked for closer study. So far we have located four previously unrecognised galago species in Tanzania alone, and we are extending the scope of our work to other parts of Africa.

3. INTRODUCTION:
 It is believed that the often fragmented and isolated nature of these forests has led to a radiation of both the flora and fauna that they contain The initial findings of this project show that the galagos of these forests are no exception, and in some mas may conmbute to the extraordinary levels of endemism encountered.
Abstract: Galagos, or bushbabies are nocturnal prosimian primates which are very widely distributed in Africa south of the Sahara. For many years only six different species were recognised but, by 1986, most authorities accepted eleven species. The reason for this discrepency was that some species looked almost identical but were found to diverge quite dramatically in their 'mate recognityion systems' (calling patterns, reproductive anatomy, chemical communication etc.). As a result they did not interbreed. This project forms part of a larger programme designed to: a) update the taxonomy of nocturnal cryptic primates; b) reconstruct patterns of speciation and geographical expansion or contraction; c) identify sites of special interest and importance for conservation; d) indicate priorities for sites and species; and d) encourage conservation initiatives and training at a grass-roots level. Our fieldwork set out to examine the genetic and ecological distinctions between a number of galago populations, including species which shared the same habitat. We are now in the process of comparing mitochondrial DNA from blood and hair samples to gain a measure of genetic distance between populations, as well as an idea of genetic variabliity within isolated and non-isolated populations. At the same time we have developed a powerful behavioural disciminator called 'vocal fingerprinting'. Each species is found to have a distinctive set of loud calls that remains more-or-less constant from one population to another irrespective of the extent of its geographical range. Quantitative measurement of call structure from sonagrams and oscillograms gives an independent measure of variation between populations and provides a highly practical way of identifying divergent forms and previously unrecognised species. In the last twelve months this work has produced dramatic results that we had not anticipated. Populations that we initially identified as allopatric forms of the same species turned out to be distinct species, with diagnostic differences in their vocal fingerprints, habitat use and behaviour. Several of the forests contained species that we did not recognise, and these have been earmarked for closer study. So far we have located four previously unrecognised galago species in Tanzania alone, and we are extending the scope of our work to other parts of Africa. The main tasks now are to characterise and name each different species; to distinguish those which are new to science from those which have been wrongly lumped together under existing specific and subspecific names; and to assess their conservation status.

Paper Title: Identification, distribution, taxonomy, and conservation status of three populations of galagos (Primates, Galagidae) in Malawi.
Relevant Excerpt:
1. Abstract:
 Identification, distribution, taxonomy, and conservation status of three populations of galagos (Primates, Galagidae) in Malawi.

2. Objectives:
 This information is closely linked to assessments of biodiversity, but is also vital for understanding galago ecology and habitat requirements, leading to a better understanding of the geographic range of each taxon and providing a basis for future conservation management plans.

3. Study subjects:
 Three widely separated galagos are thought to be endemic to Malawi:
Abstract:
Galagos (Primates, Galagidae) are small (150 – 2000 grams), nocturnal primates, that can be found over most parts of sub-Saharan Africa (Groves 2001; Grubb et al. 2003). While a few species are very widely distributed, most are confined to small and isolated regions and long term research is limited to only four countries (South Africa, Tanzania, Kenya, and Gabon - Nekaris & Bearder 2007; Grubb et al. 2003). The IUCN Red List (IUCN 2006) lists only 62.5% of the galago species that are now recognised (15 of 24 - Grubb et al. 2003) and two taxa (Galagoides sp. nov. 3 and G. rondoensis) are listed by the IUCN/SSC Primate Specialist Group among the Top 25 Most Endangered Primates (Mittermeier et al. 2006a, b). The lack of research on this group is, therefore, especially alarming. There is an urgent need to conduct more baseline and long-term research on cryptic species that are currently overlooked in relation to conservation protection programmes, and to widen the geographical coverage of such efforts.
Malawi is believed to have five species of galagos, of which two (Galagoides nyasae and G. sp. nov 1) are not listed in the IUCN Red List and one (Galagoides granti) is registered as Data Deficient (IUCN 2006). The only previous scientific studies of galagos in Malawi are two short term field studies (Courtenay & Bearder 1989; Wallace 2005) limited to very small geographical areas. With a current annual deforestation rate in Malawi at 3.5% (WWF 2006) it is clear that all forest species now face severe threats to their future existence. Establishment of a scientific baseline on the presence and abundance of key indicator species will enhance recognition of the problem and help to establish protection strategies for the most vulnerable and important of the remaining forest habitats.

Paper Title: SPECIATION AMONG GALAGOS (PRIMATES, GALAGIDAE) IN TANZANIAN FORESTS
Relevant Excerpt:

 This galago of uncertain taxonomic status  appears to have a distribution that is limited to areas of southeastern Tanzania ;

2. 77).:
 As part of the discussion of the conservation of the habitat of Tanzanian galagos, it is ' necessary to examine the different levels of legal protection afforded to Tanzania forests.

3. THE CONSERVATION OF TANZANIAN GALAGOS AND THEIR:
 "Low Risk: The species faces a low risk of extinction at the present time. It has a wide dishibution and broad ecological tolerances and a total population is probably >100,000."
Abstract: Past field studies on nocturnal primates in Africa have focused on species occupying woodland in southern Africa and primary rain forest in West and Central Africa. Relatively little is known about the galagos (Primates, Galagidae) inhabiting the fragmented, evergreen forests of East Africa. The development of new field techniques, coupled with technological advances has enabled more accurate identification of species based on species-specific calls. Nocturnal surveys during five periods of field work (July-August, 1990; August-November, 1992; February-May, 1993; July-November, 1993; July-November, 1994) in isolated coastal and Eastern Arc Mountain forests in Tanzania indicate that considerable speciation has taken place among galagos in these forest 'islands'. The loud calls of galagos, recorded during surveys and computer analysed, provide a vocal profile that is consistent within species and which can be used in a taxonomic context to identify species. This approach, founded on an examination of a major element of the Specific-Mate Recognition System (Recognition Concept of Species: Paterson, 1985), has resulted in the identification and naming of 4 new species of galago in Tanzania (Galagoides granti, G. udzungwensis, G. orinus, G. rondoensis). The identification of these species is supported by evidence of differences in other aspects of vocalisations as well as in reproductive and hair morphology and lifestyles. These discoveries suggest the true number of species of both galagos and other secretive, nocturnal, forest-dwelling mammals may have been seriously underestimated. At the start of this study 11 galago species were recognised (Nash et al., 1989), by the end this has increased to at least 17. These species are placed in a new classification of the family Galagidae, based on vocal profile differences. An assessment of the conservation threat status of Tanzanian galago indicates that the 4 new species are all of at least IUCN Red List Vulnerable status. This is primarily due to restricted distribution in small, fragmented and isolated habitats which themselves are under considerable threat. Recommendations are made concerning the conservation of galagos and their habitats. Increasing knowledge of the high levels of endemism in the evergreen forests of Tanzania further highlights the biological value of these areas in a region where considerable conservation effort is often restricted to savannah habitats and their large mammal occupants.

Paper Title: Galago gallarum
Author Names:
Thomas M. Butynski
Yvonne A. de Jong
Relevant Excerpt:
1. Conservation:
 One of the most widely distributed and adaptable of all the galagos. Not in danger of extinction.

2. Conservation:
 In addition, these galagos are killed because they are considered an agricultural pest in several areas and, on Inguja I., they are looked on as symbols of bad luck and evil .

3. Geographic Variation:
 Galago moholi. Northern limit is L. Victoria, central Tanzania, and N Mozambique. Smaller.
Abstract: Medium-sized galago with whitish face and throat, and contrasting dark brown eye-rings, and black ears and tail. Loud advertisement call distinctive. Sexes alike in colour. No data available on sexual dimorphism, but males probably slightly (10-20%) heavier than females as in other Galago spp. (Izard & Nash 1988, Harcourt & Bearder 1989). [Olson has some sexual dimorphism data.] Head broad. Muzzle short, almost black over distal half. Nose black, slightly up-turned at tip. Chin light pink. Face, throat, chest and belly pelage off-white, with grayish tint on belly. Interocular stripe off-white, broad. Eye-rings dark brown, narrow, sometimes incomplete laterally. Black ‘tear line' from inner corner of eyes down side of muzzle. Eyelids greyish. Eyes large, orange-brown. Eye shine bright orange. Ears bare, shiny black skin front and dull black skin back. Dorsal pelage short (not woolly), variable, from light sandy brown to buff to fulvous, lighter on head. Dorsal hairs dark grey over basal half, buff near middle followed by whitish sub terminal band and blackish tip. Shoulders buff or light rufous. Outer forelimbs and hands buff to sandy-brown. Hips, outer hindlimbs and feet yellowish-buff. Digits blackish. Tail mostly black but dark brown or reddish towards base. Hand (volar) pads distinctive (Anderson 1999). Young similar to adult in colour.

Paper Title: GEOGRAPHIC VARIATION IN THE LOUDCALLS OF TREE HYRAX – DENDROHYRAX VALIDUS (TRUE 1890) – IN THE EASTERN ARC MOUNTAINS, EAST AFRICA: TAXONOMIC AND CONSERVATION IMPLICATIONS
Publication Title: UNIVERSITY OF READING MSc Wildlife Management: Conservation and Control
Relevant Excerpt:
1. BIOGEOGRAPHY:
 Such conditions have resulted in high levels of speciation, as evinced by the large number of galago species endemic to the forests of Tanzania (Honess 1996).
Abstract: Extensive studies have used bioacoustics in defining the taxonomy and systematics of various taxa, especially nocturnal and cryptic animals that use vocal sounds (and often olfactory signals) as their primary means of intra-specific communication. Recent research revealed distinct inter-specific differences in vocalisation patterns of tree hyrax species [Hyracoidea, Procavidae, Dendrohyrax, (Kingdon, 1997)]. Extensive geographical variation in the vocalisations of the eastern tree hyrax., Dendrohyrax validus (True 1890), occurring in disjunct populations in the montane and coastal forests of eastern Tanzania and southern Kenya, was also revealed. Based on data from recordings obtained from sixteen forest populations (populations) within four isolated mountain blocks and two oceanic islands (regions), this study identified three distinct call-zones on the basis of marked qualitative differences in the vocal profiles of D. validus occurring in (1) the islands of Zanzibar and Pemba, (2) the Uluguru and Udzungwa Mountains, Tanzania, (3) the East Usambara Mountains, Tanzania and Taita Hills, Kenya. Within each call-zone, further population differences were analysed. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) tests were used to investigate significant differences in call patterns between (1) paired regions and (2) forest populations within each region. The taxonomic implications are discussed. It is proposed that at least three distinct D. validus sub-populations occur, subject to further behavioural and genetic research. In addition, significant inter-regional and inter-population variation was found. These findings have important conservation implications in relation to loss of biodiversity through population loss in isolated, fragmented forest habitats.

Paper Title: Loud Calls of Galago crassicaudatus and G. garnettii and their Relation to Habitat Structure
Relevant Excerpt:
1. AIMS:
 This study formed part of a broader investigation of the genetic status of the greater galagos . Its primary aim was to identify and quantify the differences between the calls of the taxa, and to relate these to the habitats in which they occur.
Abstract: Long distance vocalizations have been shown to be good indicators of genetic species in primates. Here the loud calls of two recently identified greater galago taxa - Galago crassicaudatus and G. garnettii - are compared and analyzed statistically. Observed differences in call structures are investigated further as potential indicators of differences in the structures of habitats frequented by the two species. Although the calls share a repetitive structure, and show similar dominant frequency bands (1,000-1,500 Hz), they differ significantly in the number of units per call, unit duration, inter-unit interval, highest frequency, lowest frequency, dominant frequency band, first harmonic, and call duration. The duration of the G. crassicaudatus call is more than twice that of G. garnettii. Strong intraspecific consistency is seen in the most energetic frequency bands (dominant frequency band and first harmonic), and durations of the individual units and inter-unit intervals. Information important to species recognition is thus most likely to be contained in these features. Individual recognition may be encoded in the relative emphasis of higher level harmonics. The frequency structures of the calls will reflect requirements for acoustical transmission in a forest environment, as well as structural constraints imposed by body size. Higher frequencies detected in the G. garnettii call (up to 8,500 Hz) may have a functional significance related to distance estimation, or may simply be a reflection of smaller body size. The greater modulation of the G. garnettii call suggests that its habitat constitutes a denser or more turbulent medium for sound transmission than does the habitat of G. crassicaudatus.

Paper Title: A survey of nocturnal primates in Malawi – August 2009
Relevant Excerpt:
1. Introduction:
 Further poorly known species have been provisionally identified in southern Malawi -in the region of Mount Mulanje and Thyolo Mountains (Galagoides nyasae and Galagoides sp. nov. 2).

2. Abstract:
 We collected over 10 hours of tape recordings of galago calls, 4 hours of video recordings and many photographs of the animals and their habitats to aid a more detailed analysis and assessment of conservation priorities for the severely threatened, yet unique forest areas that remain in Malawi.

3. Results:
 Photographs and tape recordings enabled us to confirm that the very small bushbaby common in this forest was a montane galago not previously recorded for Malawi, but studied by Perkin & Bearder in the nearby Mount Rungwe, Tanzania, in 2005.
Abstract: We conducted 75 hours of nocturnal surveys in the rainforests of Malawi from 30 July to 23 August 2009 in three regions: Mount Mulanje, in the south; Nkhata Bay, on the lake-shore; and in the Misuku Hills, in the far north. We identified four different kinds of bushbaby (galagos) on the basis of their different calls and pelage colouration: Otolemur crassicaudatus (in all areas); Galagoides granti (Southern Malawi); Galagoides sp. nov. 1 (near Nkahata Bay); and Galagoides orinus/Galagoides sp. nov. 3 (Mughese Forest, Misuku). We noted the position, height and behaviour of each animal seen during night surveys using torch light and a handheld GPS-unit. We collected over 10 hours of tape recordings of galago calls, 4 hours of video recordings and many photographs of the animals and their habitats to aid a more detailed analysis and assessment of conservation priorities for the severely threatened, yet unique forest areas that remain in Malawi. The complete destruction of the Kalwe Forest Reserve, the last remaining easily accessible forest of its kind in Malawi and a national treasure, is particularly alarming.

Paper Title: Identification, distribution, and taxonomy of three populations of galagos (Primates, Galagidae) in Malawi
Relevant Excerpt:
1. Abstract:
 The information that is gathered will serve as baseline data for a PhDproject commencing during the winter 20078, on the phylogeny and biogeography of galagos in the southern regions of the Eastern Afromontane Biodiversity Hotspot ).
Abstract: The objectives of this study are: 1. To establish a preliminary taxonomic rank of three of the most isolated and esoteric populations of galagos (bushbabies) (Primates, Galagidae) of the genus Galagoides in Africa: (Malawi galago, G. nyasae; Kalwe small galago, G. sp. nov. 1; Misuku Hills galago, G. sp.), based on their vocal repertoire. 2. To investigate the ecological requirements of these populations. 3. To estimate local population density and distribution with each type locality. 4. To initiate biogeographic mapping of the inhabited forest patches using Geographic Information System (GIS) models. The information that is gathered will serve as baseline data for a PhD-project commencing during the winter 2007-8, on the phylogeny and biogeography of galagos in the southern regions of the Eastern Afromontane Biodiversity Hotspot (Brooks et al. 2007).

Paper Title: Cryptic diversity and species boundaries within the Paragalago zanzibaricus species complex
Author Names:
Luca Pozzi
Anna Penna
Simon K. Bearder
Johan Karlsson
Andrew Perkin
Todd R. Disotell
Relevant Excerpt:
1. Conclusions:
 The diversity within this genus both genetically and acoustically underlines the importance of combining molecular tools, natural history and behavioral information to elucidate the evolutionary history of dwarf galagos.

2. Introduction:
 The African galagids (Primates; Galagidae) represent another primate group where species diversity has been long underestimated due to the lack of clear morphological variation .
Abstract: The recently described genus Paragalago is a complex of several nocturnal and morphologically cryptic species distributed in the forests of eastern Africa. Species diversity within this genus has been mainly described using species-specific differences in their loud calls. However, molecular data are still lacking for this group and species boundaries remain unclear. In this study, we explore species diversity within the zanzibaricus-complex using a combination of mitochondrial and nuclear data and comparing multiple species delimitation methods. Our results consistently support the existence of three independent lineages, P. cocos, P. zanzibaricus, and P. granti, confirming previous hypotheses based on vocal data. We conclude that these three lineages represent valid cryptic species and we hypothesize that speciation within this complex was characterized by cycles of forest expansion and contraction in the Plio-Pleistocene.

Paper Title: Molecular Phylogeny of East African Dwarf Galagos (Galagidae; Galagoides)
Publication Title: Primate Society of Great Britain RESEARCH GRANT Application Form

1. Aims:
 We therefore suggest a survey of specific localities in Tanzania to collect genetic material from four species of dwarf galago (table ). In combination with samples derived from previous surveys, the intent is to construct the most comprehensive molecular phylogeny made to date.

2. Aims:
 We believe the results from this study will prove pivotal for future research and conservation prioritizing of dwarf galagos.
Abstract: As with many nocturnal animals, dwarf galagos (Galagoides spp.) have a cryptic morphology that confounds full understanding of their true species diversity. To fully quantify the extent of the taxonomic variety of the genus, it is necessary to conduct an assessment of the genetic diversity in an evolutionary perspective, i.e. create a genetically based phylogeny. However, current records of dwarf galago DNA are very patchy and are mostly derived from captive populations, leaving incomplete phylogenetic understanding. We therefore suggest a survey of specific localities in Tanzania to collect genetic material from four species of dwarf galago (table 1). In combination with samples derived from previous surveys, the intent is to construct the most comprehensive molecular phylogeny made to date. We believe the results from this study will prove pivotal for future research and conservation prioritizing of dwarf galagos.

Relevant Excerpt:
1. Abstract:

2. Introduction:
 A detailed study by  provided strong evidence that the small galagos in this area were Galagoides granti, as found in neighbouring Mozambique and extending north into coastal Tanzania.
Abstract: We conducted 78 hours of nocturnal surveys in the rainforests of Malawi from 30 July to 23 August 2009 in three regions: Mount Mulanje, in the south; Nkhata Bay, on the lake-shore; and in the Misuku Hills, in the far north. We identified four different kinds of bushbaby (galagos) on the basis of their different calls and pelage colouration: Otolemur crassicaudatus (in all areas); Galagoides granti (Southern Malawi); Galagoides sp. nov. 1 (near Nkahata Bay); and Galagoides orinus/Galagoides sp. nov. 3 (Mughesse Forest, Misuku Hills Forest Reserve). We noted the position, height and behaviour of each animal seen during night surveys using torch light and a handheld GPS-unit. We collected over 10 hours of tape recordings of galago calls, 4 hours of video recordings and many photographs of the animals and their habitats to aid a more detailed analysis and assessment of conservation priorities for the severely threatened, yet unique forest areas that remain in Malawi. The complete destruction of the Kalwe Forest Reserve, the last remaining easily accessible forest of its kind in Malawi and a national treasure, is particularly alarming.

Paper Title: Phylogenetic Relationships Among the Galaginae as Indicated by Erythrocytic Allozymes
Author Names:
J. C. Masters
R. J. Rayner
H. Ludewick
E. Zimmermann
N. Molez-Verriere
F. Vincent
L. T. Nash
Relevant Excerpt:
1. Abstract:
 The data also show strong concordance with results obtained using highly repeated DNA sequences, which indicate that the galagos form a close-knit genetic group, while the Malagasy lemurids show considerably more inter-taxic variation.
Abstract: At least four hypotheses regarding the phylogenetic relationships within the Galaginae have been proposed, based on features of karyology, morphology, and behaviour, but these hypotheses share few common elements. Here we investigate erythrocytic allozymes as potential phylogenetic markers, and subject our results to a cladistic analysis. Our study offers little support for the previous models, but suggests instead that the greater galagos and G. alleni form a clade since they share character states for eight of the ten systems examined. The two lesser galago species could not be distinguished using these enzymes, and character states common to them and the alleni-crassicaudatus-garnettii clade were found in only four systems. Our resultant cladogram accords well with the rather scant galagine fossil record. The data also show strong concordance with results obtained using highly repeated DNA sequences, which indicate that the galagos form a close-knit genetic group, while the Malagasy lemurids show considerably more inter-taxic variation.

Paper Title: Identification, distribution, and taxonomy of three endemic populations of galagos (Primates, Galagidae, Galagoides spp.) in Malawi.
Relevant Excerpt:
1. Abstract:
 Identification, distribution, and taxonomy of three endemic populations of galagos (Primates, Galagidae, Galagoides spp.) in Malawi.
Abstract: The cryptic, nocturnal, and vocal African primates of the genus Galagoides (Primates, Galagidae), belong, not only to the smallest (<200 grams) subset within its family, but also to one of the least studied. As an effect of a paradigm shift in species concept twenty years ago, to the Recognition Species Concept, galago vocalizations became useful as species denominator. The very elusive Galagoides then became an easier target for study, and as a result, approximately six new Galagoides species was described. In some cases the rate of discovery surpassed the rate of detailed species descriptions which lead to ambiguities in taxonomic status of several the new taxa. Malawi harbour three esoteric and little studied endemic populations of Galagoides: Malawi galago (Galagoides nyasae); Kalwe small galago (Galagoides sp. nov. 1); and the Misuku Hills galago (Galagoides sp.). There exists little or no records of their vocal repertoire and their status has not been confirmed in 20-100 years. Hence, his study suggests a survey of their type localities to record each population's vocal repertoire, mapping their geographical distribution, local abundance, and establish their basic ecological preferences. The findings from this project are expected to give strong indications on their taxonomic and ecological status, and further underpin a planned long-term study of Malawi's galagos.
